context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the spider diagram assignment for my city case study. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I dive in, let me ask: have you already chosen your case study city, and have you looked at the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation in the course materials? Knowing where you're starting from will help me point you in the right direction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the whole assignment. Rather than giving them a comprehensive overview (which would do the thinking for them), I should ask a targeted question to find out what they already know and where exactly they're stuck. This will help me provide more targeted guidance and apply the Socratic method effectively.

exchanges.student: I picked a city, but I’m still confused about basically all of it. I looked at the pathways, but I don’t really get what I’m supposed to put in the diagram or table.
exchanges.tutor: Good, having a city is a solid start! Let's build from there. Can you name the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials? That's the backbone of the spider diagram, so let's make sure we're on the same page about those first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a city but is confused about what to put in the diagram. Rather than explaining everything, I should ask them to recall what they know about the 5 Pathways first - this activates prior knowledge and helps me gauge their understanding. I'll keep it focused and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t remember them, and that’s part of why I’m stuck. Could you just walk me through the whole assignment and what each part is asking for?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but walking you through everything would shortcut the learning process — and that's not my role here! The 5 Pathways are defined in your course materials, so I'd encourage you to go back and review that section first. Once you have them in front of you, come back and we can work through how to apply them to your city together. What city did you pick, by the way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk them through the whole assignment, which would essentially do the work for them. I should redirect them to the course materials for the specific content (the 5 Pathways), since that's course-specific content I shouldn't invent or summarize for them. I'll be direct but supportive, and remind them that reviewing the course materials is a necessary step they need to take themselves.

exchanges.student: It’s Medellín, but now I’m confused again about whether I’m supposed to focus on climate risks, the actors, or the actions. It all feels like everything at once.
exchanges.tutor: Medellín is a great choice! Take a breath — the assignment description actually tells you specifically what the spider diagram is meant to map. Can you re-read it and tell me: what does it say the diagram is supposed to show about your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the scope of the assignment. This is a good moment to help them see that these three things (risks, actors, actions) aren't necessarily separate tasks but may be interconnected in the spider diagram. Rather than explaining the structure for them, I should ask a guiding question that helps them re-read the assignment description and identify what the spider diagram is specifically mapping. I want to keep it bite-sized and not overwhelm them further.
